sentences.text: Teacher.
Yeah, she has the-- she said to us that you are sitting there like the Jews in a mikveh.
So I said-- I stood up and I said, I don't belong here.
And this was not allowed to do it because this was a special place, that you have to stay there.
And I said, I can't do what you want to do.
I went again to the director and told him the story.
And this is politic.
And it was not allowed to belong to organization, even, when you went to high school because school is not a political ground to-- especially to children, you know?
And I said, I'm not going back to there, even you're going to dismiss me from the school.
He said, don't worry about-- we're going to talk about that.
And he-- I didn't go.
Would you explain for us what she meant when she said that?
Because there was-- you have a-- a very heavy rifle in you hands.
You couldn't hold it.
And then everyone was lying like, you know, really very bad.
She can said it in another way.
Come-- I know it's very heavy.
Come stood up, and I show you how to do it.
That's it.
The meaning of the word--
Mikveh is a-- a ritual bath.
In a Orthodox shul, they have a-- a-- a-- like a swimming-- a swimming pool, but in a smaller thing.
And everyone, woman and man, when he had to have intercourse, he had to go to the mikveh, to this thing, and-- and take a bath.
And she also take a bath-- not together, but separate.
Man is separate woman.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
She-- she's trying to say that we cannot fight.
They had the idea that the Jews cannot fight.
When the-- the independent day in-- in May in 1948 in Poland, it was the communist country, communist government.
They try-- they helped the Jews in Israel to became a independent country in the beginning, the Russians.

They thought that the Jews are going to be communist, and they're going to have another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another country to aggravate the rest of the-- of the world, especially the Americans and the-- and the European free countries.
And so-- but the Jews didn't want to.
They want to-- they know a lot of the Jews who belonged to [INAUDIBLE] Israel, the-- the parliament there, that they were in Russia, and they were the same thing-- very bad treated there.
They-- they-- they didn't say that you Jewish, but they were treated like-- like the worst kind, like-- like Hitler.
Yeah.
This was in May in 1939.
And I was home.
And in September 1, I supposed to go to Warsaw, and to be accepted to the dental school.
But September 1, they started bombing our city, and I didn't go.
I stayed home for the two, three months, how--

No, because a-- a few days-- a few weeks before the invasion of Poland, Chamberlain and-- and Daladier from France-- and Chamberlain from England-- they came back from a convention-- a conference with Hitler.
And they had a written statement that they will-- he's not-- he doesn't want a war.
He doesn't want anything.
And the only thing he wants, he wants Sudetendeutschland, to take a piece of-- of-- piece of Czechoslovakia.
And then he's not going anywhere.
I was in--
It's-- I was-- I-- I took a bath.
And I was in my robe.
And they started bombing.
My mother was on the market.
It was Tuesday-- or Friday.
Friday, because every time she goes on, it was Friday.
And Mother went to market, you know?
And-- and I was prepared [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to wait for mother to make breakfast.
And then mother came.
And she said, out from the house.
And we went to a shelter.
And I was in-- only in this robe--
--in the shelter.
My mother and my father.
My father was the commandant of anti-- what do you call?
Anti-- anti-- air-- air-- anti-air-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, they-- they--
No, they didn't-- he didn't fight.
But he was to tell people where to go, to tell you-- he was in a another block, not in our block.
In another block.
My brother and my sister.
With us, yeah.
To the shelters.
It was every big house, because we live in a very big house.
And they have their own shelters.
And there are the-- they are-- the neighbors used to come to our shelter.
And there-- there you can see that everyone was very good to each other because it was a tragedy.
A lot of people were praying.
I prayed with them together too.
It didn't help, but I prayed.
About a hour.
They bomb only the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- not the railroad tracks, because they need it for transportation.
They bomb only the chemical factories.
They were a little further from the tracks there.
And-- and-- and they have a lot of-- of spies in the factory because this was ammunition factory, or it was-- belongs to the Germans, to the French, to our government, too.
And, you know, they have those Germans.
They-- they know everything what's going on in the factories.
Came home.
And we wait for-- for-- my father came home.
He demobilized everything.
We-- we escaped to a village, to the-- to the peasant who used to bring milk from-- not far from the city.
And we stood there a whole week.
Yeah, about the third day of war.
It was September 3 until 8.
Every night, every second night, we used to go home and bring out the-- what we have in the house, because we left the house in a hurry.
We didn't take with us anything else.
And so we came to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to this peasant's there.
And there, hundreds of thousand soldiers were walking away, back-- and they told us where they-- from where they escaping there.
Thousands of soldiers ran through this thing.
My father used to go to the peasant and buy milk for those soldiers.
They didn't have what to eat.
They didn't have no organized-- nothing because the rest left Poland.
The big shots left Poland.
And they left those soldiers who fight to the last-- to the last drop of-- of blood.
From Poland, yeah.
A lot of Jews, a lot of Poles.
It was-- they went to Warsaw.
I saw them walking away.
They didn't know where they were going to be.
But we know that they-- they were fighting for about three weeks.
And after three weeks, they-- they came over, and that's it.
The soldier?
They tell that this was soldiers who are heroes.
Half of them died.
Half of them died.
During the-- the fight with the Germans.
You know?
Yeah.
No.
No.
I-- we stayed there a hour, maybe.
And we came home.
And then we left.
To the-- to the village.
About eight-- eight days.
But every night, we came home, or every second night.
We take the-- the luggage we left in the house, because people come and they loot it.
They came-- one time, we came home and they tried to open our closets.
And they were special keys that you cannot open it like that.
And he stand up to take the chandelier out of-- and my father came in.
And he started screaming that he's going to throw to the balcony out.
My father.
My father, because he was so enraged.
He lost the-- the-- he lost the freedom.
And you come to a house and rob.
Some from-- villagers.
You know?
We didn't know.
And I begged my father, leave it alone.
Leave it alone.
It's not worth it.
Open the door, throw them out from the house.
And so we came every night to take something else.
But the chandelier, he wanted to take with.
With our peasant wife who used to bring us milk from her--
To the house.
Yeah, in her home.
The-- they have-- it was a dirty house because the cow was in the house-- not in the house, but almost in the house.
Fleas, the terrible fleas.
So we ask her that we want to sleep at the attic.
Maybe there, there'll be less-- [LAUGHS] less flies.
We slept in the attic later.
The whole family.
You know?
So then--
Yeah, we find--
--a-- a-- a old man there who was working by my great-grandfather.
They were very rich that time.
So they have a lot of children.
And he told us how beautiful my great-grandmother was.
The-- the--
Great-- great-grandfather.
Yeah.
Yeah, there-- a old peasant--
--living there in the house, yeah--
--not far in the village.
And he-- remember, my father used to like to listen to those old people tales or something like that.
And he told us he met my mother's side of the family, it was.
Yeah, Skarzysko-Koscielny.
It means that they have a church there from that-- this was bigger than Skarzysko-Kamienna.
But Skarzysko-Kamienna became bigger because they have a lot of factories.
They have-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] they have a railroad station in the middle.
Everything, who you go from east to west, you have to go to Skarzysko-Kamienna, you see?
It was very, very famous about that.
The-- this--
--village?
5 kilometers.
Yeah, we walked.
And a funny thing.
My sister was four years old that time.
And she was holding her nose and said, father, can I leave my nose alone?

[LAUGHS] Because, you see, we were-- they teach us, however, it's going to be a gas, because we taught-- they taught that Hitler is throwing gas too, and to have something wet to-- to hold your breath, not to breathe.
So she had those things and she hold her nose, my four-year-old sister.
We came home.
And then the Germans took over.
My father went to the-- the Germans there.
And he said that the people are starving already, and to give him permission to bring some food for them.
And so he gave him permission to go and to bring the food.
They were-- they-- they kill you with kindness.
And a few times, they confiscated his-- everything what he bought, and took away from him.
The Germans?
Yeah, about a few days later, after we came back home.
It was about the 15th of-- of September, [INAUDIBLE].
You know?
They organized a health department in the-- in the-- it was not a ghetto yet-- to heal those people.
But we didn't have any medication there.
Dr.
Cahan, the physician, and Doctor-- his wife was a dentist.
And she said to my father and mother that I can take Dora as a assistant to her in-- in the office--
--and-- and registered us as a maid, like a-- a-- a hired place to be in this health department.
So this way, they're not going to touch me, and-- and I'm working fine.
The olders-- the elders, Jewish community.
And they-- they organized.
They-- they know before maybe what was in Germany, that those-- those-- those health departments were stayed longer, and longer, and longer.
You know?
And they always thought that this would survive-- the Skarzysko ghetto would survive, and everything was surviving.
Yeah.
Mm-hmm.
I-- I went and I helped my-- the-- the dentist-- doctor.
And she told me what to do.
And I-- I helped her a lot.
Prepare the-- this was different than now, you know?
Technique was completely different.
To prep the patient, talk to the patient, to tell her it's going to hurt a little bit, it's not going to hurt a little bit.
And so it depends what kind of patient you have.
And so little by little, I did-- she even asked me to subtract a-- her tooth.
I said no.
So-- [LAUGHS] you know?
And one day, when the ghetto was-- you know, after they confiscate everything and after they took away everything from the Jews, the doctor-- Dr.
Cahan and her husband came.
And he said, you know, that we need about 200 girls to go to war-- to go to war-- to the ammunition factory.
And I need a name, he said to me.
And your name would be the nicest thing, because a lot of people will go where you go.
You know?
And so I said, OK.
And I will take you out after the-- the rest go to work.
I will take you back, because you work in a health department.
So I went.
And then they ask-- the-- the boss, a sadist-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what-- his name was Herring.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[INAUDIBLE] was.
He was the worst kind of a person.
A young man, about 28 years old.
Tall, SS.
And he asked if-- who speaks German.
And some said, Dora, Dora.
Who's Dora?
And I said in Polish, shut up.
I don't want to go.
I don't want to be known to you.
So he couldn't-- he couldn't say otherwise.
So he took me there.
And he asked me to write down the names, 200 girls' names.
And the girls were cleaning the machines, you know?
The ammunition-- You mean that was-- you mean that was their--
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah, to clean the thing.
And I used to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to put the names in writing.
And the next day, Dr.
Cahan Came over.
And he asked me how was it.
I said, thank you for being so nice to me.
But I'm not going back.
He said, don't go back.
The next day, 7 o'clock in the morning, the commandant of the-- of the camp came in.
I was in my nightgown, in my robe.
In the house.
And he came and picked me up like-- I stood like that-- back to the-- to the office there.
Yeah.
And I was sitting there 12 hours, doing nothing.
Can you imagine to sit in there doing nothing?
Yeah.
Yeah, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
--because that way, we can save the ghetto.
So to save the ghetto, you go.
Nothing.
Nothing.
I obeyed everyone who was a-- a human being, and they were a human being.
This man used to go to the very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] poor people and help them.
The doctor--
Doctor--
Doctor--
Dr.
Cahan, the-- the physicist.
The-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] yeah.
No, he didn't--
No, they-- we had to go in the morning, 5 o'clock in the morning, to go and to wait until some Germans would take you--
--to walk.
It was the-- yeah.
With the 200 girls.
We walked.
5-- 5 kilometers.
The munition factory, yeah.
And--
We-- the-- the commandant?
The commandant was very, very nice.
He-- but he was crazy about children, and he killed adults.
Can you understand those things?
He loves children.
He took the children.
He had a few children in his-- when somebody supposed to come to-- for a visitation in the camp, he took the children to his palace, and he feed them.
In Skarzysko-Kamienna.
Yeah.
And he killed adults.
He goes and-- he saw when somebody was sleeping-- not in the barracks.
But in the barracks, we have also a lot of everything, what you want-- rats and the bed-- bed bunks, you know?
Those fly-- you know, we have bunks three-- three bunks.
Not two, but three.
And no food.
Yeah, yeah.

I work-- I worked through the-- same time until '41-'42, until the complete liquidation, because they-- they couldn't-- they didn't have enough transportation to-- put the 7,000 people or 10,000 people.
They have to have it a time.
They liquidated our ghetto from Rosh Hashanah to Sukkot.
To Treblinka.
It was about April.
Was-- it was cold.
So cold.
And we was-- we walked.
We had to go through a little forest.
And one time-- [INAUDIBLE] was the main SS man in this factory-- factories.
Three-- it was three factories.
And he had a son.
And he had two big dogs, you know?
The German shepherd.
And when we walked, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he let the dogs go.
And-- and he jump.
And I said, you run.
I will stay because I like dogs, and they will stay with me.
And they came over.
And they-- he beat me in my behind.
And that's it.
